result:
I want to write about the moment the room goes quiet after the last page of a book is turned and the cover closes with a soft, papery exhale. It is the same hush that follows the last chord of a nocturne when the piano strings are still trembling like damp laundry in winter wind, or the instant after a kettle clicks off and the foam subsides, leaving only the ceramic to remember the boil. These tiny suspensions—call them the parentheses of being—are where I keep finding myself lately, as though life has become a long ellipsis made of ordinary miracles that refuse to advertise themselves.

My grandmother used to collect silence the way other women amassed porcelain cats or commemorative teaspoons. She stored it in odd places: the sewing basket, the pocket of her raincoat, the space between the humming refrigerator and the wall. When I visited, she would open a cupboard and hand me a scoop of hush—“Here, taste this,” she’d say, as if quiet were a spice. I thought she was eccentric; now I think she was an alchemist. Because when the world is loud in the way only the twenty-first century can be—notifications jumping out like pop-up jack-in-the-boxes, engines arguing over lane changes, podcasts bleeding from other people’s earbuds—silence becomes a contraband item, an endangered mineral smuggled across the borders of our attention.

I read once that astronauts returning from orbit sometimes experience a condition called “Earth Overload Syndrome.” After months in the hush of vacuum, the simple rustle of leaves sounds to them like a drum kit dropped down a stairwell. They report standing in supermarkets overwhelmed by the fluorescent chatter, the beeping registers, the colors screaming off boxes of cereal. They flee to parking lots, sit on asphalt, press palms to their ears—not to block sound, but to cradle the memory of zero-g tranquility. I think of them whenever I exit a shopping mall and find the sky has turned a bruised violet while I was inside, bargain-hunting under halogen suns. We are all cosmonauts of our personal space stations, spinning wildly, catching snippets of whatever calm drifts past the porthole.

Tonight the moon looks like a nickel pressed to the dark thumb of the sky, and I am walking the dog beneath its counterfeit glow. The dog—her name is Ardor, because she is earnest about everything—keeps stopping to read the news of urine on clumps of grass. Each sniff is a novella to her, a soap opera starring last night’s fox and the trembling ghost of a squirrel. I stand patiently, pretending I’m not also deciphering headlines: the distant barking of someone’s quarrel, the Doppler sigh of a passing cyclist, the faint smell of laundry detergent from a dryer vent that reminds me of a kindergarten classroom twenty-eight years ago, crayons stubbed like tiny torches. Memory, I’ve decided, is just another sense—right after smell, right before balance—and it is activated by whatever the night hands us.

A thousand words is a curious unit of measurement. Medieval cloth merchants in Flanders used something called an “ell,” the distance from elbow to fingertip. A thousand words should surely have its own ell, perhaps the length of breath you can hold while swimming underwater across a neighborhood pool. I am somewhere midway, lungs beginning to tingle, aware the tile wall is approaching where the water turns a darker blue. The impulse is to surface too soon, to gulp air and safety, but I want to stay submerged a bit longer, where sound is muffled and time feels optional.

Yesterday I learned that trees have a slang term for human time: they call it “flash-season.” One ring, one flash. They speak in chemicals, in slow sugars, in mycorrhizal gossip. When we plant a sapling, they congratulate the parent stump in dialects of sap. They do not exactly die; they just surrender their personal calendar and join library time, the kind where every due date is negotiable. I wonder what words they would produce if given a thousand of them—maybe a single verb meaning “to photosynthesize starlight into autobiography,” conjugated across centuries.

My phone vibrates. I ignore it. Ardor finds a chicken bone left by the curb and glances up guiltily, the way teenagers do when they discover the car keys still work after midnight. I negotiate a trade for the bone with a dried blueberry, and she accepts, tail conducting an invisible orchestra. Somewhere in my pocket, the unanswered message becomes a digital comet, streaking toward the edge of a server farm in Arizona, glowing hotter the longer it goes unread. I picture it landing among thousands of other orphaned communiqués, all piled like autumn leaves waiting for a rake that never comes.

I’ve been thinking about how we translate ourselves into avatars: square photos, job titles, favorite quotes. We crop our lives the way gardeners espalier fruit trees against a wall—two dimensions, orderly, picturesque but unable to spin in the wind. Meanwhile, the interior orchard is wild, half grafted with books we never finished, love letters we rehearse in the shower, irrational fears of cracking our teeth on hidden peach pits. The discrepancies grow like scar tissue until we forget what the original limb looked like. Still, late at night, we surprise ourselves humming a lullaby whose words we believed we’d misplaced forever.

At 700 words I notice the dog has grown bored with the moon and is heading home, shoulders rolling like a sailor on leave. I follow, thinking about the anatomy of returning: the squeak of the gate, the clack of keys on foyer tile, the refrigerator humming like a monk who memorized one syllable and decided it was enough. Inside, the digital clock insists it is 11:11, a palindrome, a stutter in time’s sentence. I make a wish anyway, because ritual is the small hinge that keeps the big door swinging. I wish for the courage to leave white space in my days, to regard unscheduled hours not as failures of efficiency but as reserved seating for wonder.

Because wonder is the last currency that hasn’t been devalued by market floods. You can still trade a minute of genuine curiosity for a glimpse of something enormous, like noticing how condensation on a water glass resembles Antarctica seen from orbit, or how the squeak of your finger on a clean plate produces the same frequency as a baby star’s first pulse. Children know the exchange rate instinctively; they spend wonder recklessly, like sailors on shore leave, until we teach them to budget. Then we spend adulthood trying to remember the bankruptcy procedures for awe.

Ardor curls into a comma on the rug, implying the sentence of the day is nearing completion. I sit beside her, type the last paragraphs, breathing in the faint scent of yeast from her paws, that eternal smell of buttered popcorn that vet textbooks attribute to bacteria. Outside, a neighborhood garage door grumbles shut; inside, the thermostat clicks; on my screen, the word counter climbs like ivy toward the parapet of one thousand. Somewhere between keystrokes, silence finally arrives—no fanfare, just the soft settling of a house that has decided, for this slice of night, to keep its secrets. I close the laptop, place it on the floor, and let the parentheses close.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
